text: "Standard Access," or "Restricted"). Select the groups you want to assign to the role. Select the groups you want to assign to the role. Toggle each capability on or off to define what this role grants. Toggle each capability on or off to define what this role grants. Click “Add role.” Click “Add role.” Edit a custom role Navigate to Organization settings > Custom roles . Navigate to Organization settings > Custom roles . Click the role you want to edit. Click the role you want to edit. Update the name and groups, or toggle capabilities as needed. Update the name and groups, or toggle capabilities as needed. Click “Edit role” to save your changes. Click “Edit role” to save your changes. Changes take effect within one minute. All members in groups assigned to this role are affected. Delete a custom role Click the menu button on any custom role and select “Delete role.” Deleting a role removes its permissions from all groups it was assigned to. Members in those groups lose the permissions the role granted, unless another role in their chain also grants them. Assign groups to custom roles Custom roles are assigned to groups, not directly to individual members. To assign a group to a role: Navigate to Organization settings > Custom roles . Navigate to Organization settings > Custom roles . Click the role you want to assign. Click the role you want to assign. In the groups selector, select one or more groups. In the groups selector, select one or more groups. Click "Save." Click "Save." You can also assign custom roles when creating or editing a group in Organization settings > Groups . See Manage groups and group spend limits on Enterprise plans . How permissions combine across multiple roles If a member belongs to multiple groups with different custom roles, their permissions are additive —they get the union of all permissions from all roles in their chain. If any role grants a feature, the member has access to it. This means you can't use one role to remove a permission granted by another role. This is by design — it enables a layered approach where a base role covers common features and additional roles layer on specific capabilities. Example: A member is in two groups.
